topics.title: Getting Started with JavaScript
topics.description: Set up your environment and run your very first JavaScript program in the browser and on your computer.

lessons.title: What is JavaScript?
lessons.body: JavaScript is the programming language of the web. Every modern browser can run it, and with Node.js you can also run JavaScript outside the browser — on servers, scripts, and command-line tools. You don't need much to get started: just a browser or a small runtime install.
lessons.code:
// This is JavaScript!
console.log("Hello, world!");

lessons.title: Installing Node.js
lessons.body: Node.js lets you run JavaScript files directly on your computer. Visit https://nodejs.org and download the LTS (Long Term Support) version for your operating system. The installer adds the 'node' command to your terminal. After installing, open a terminal and verify the installation by checking the version.

lessons.title: Running JS in the browser console
lessons.body: Every browser has a built-in JavaScript console. In Chrome or Edge, press F12 (or Ctrl+Shift+J on Windows, Cmd+Option+J on Mac) to open DevTools, then click the Console tab. You can type JavaScript directly and press Enter to run it. This is the fastest way to experiment with small snippets.
lessons.code:
// Type these lines one at a time in the console:
2 + 2
"Hello" + " " + "World"
Math.random()

lessons.title: Running a .js file with Node
lessons.body: Create a file called hello.js in any text editor — VS Code is a popular free choice that offers syntax highlighting and a built-in terminal. Write your code, save the file, then open a terminal in the same folder and run it with the node command. For a deeper look at the language, check out MDN's JavaScript Guide and visit the recommended books section for beginner-friendly resources.

topics.title: Variables: var, let, const
topics.description: Understand the three ways to declare variables in JavaScript and when to use each.

lessons.title: var (avoid in modern JS)
lessons.body: var is function-scoped and hoisted to the top of its scope. It can cause unexpected bugs because you can use a variable before declaring it.
lessons.code:
var x = 10;
console.log(x); // 10

lessons.title: let (block-scoped, reassignable)
lessons.body: let is block-scoped — it only exists inside the { } where it was declared. Use let for variables whose values change.

lessons.title: const (block-scoped, binding is fixed)
lessons.body: const prevents reassigning the binding. For objects and arrays, the contents can still change. Use const by default.

lessons.title: Rule of thumb
lessons.body: Prefer const. Use let when you need to reassign. Avoid var in modern JavaScript. For a deeper dive, check out MDN's guide on Grammar and Types. Don't forget to visit the recommended books section for curated resources.

topics.title: Data Types at a Glance
topics.description: Learn the fundamental data types JavaScript offers and how to identify them using typeof.

lessons.title: The seven primitive types
lessons.body: JavaScript has seven primitive (simple) types: string, number, bigint, boolean, undefined, null, and symbol. Primitives are immutable — once created, their value cannot change. When you reassign a variable, you are replacing the value, not modifying it.

lessons.title: The typeof operator
lessons.body: Use typeof to check what type a value is. It returns a string describing the type. This is handy when debugging or when you need to handle different types differently. Note that typeof null returns 'object' — this is a well-known JavaScript quirk, not a feature.
lessons.code:
console.log(typeof "hello"); // "string"
console.log(typeof 42);      // "number"
console.log(typeof true);    // "boolean"
console.log(typeof undefined); // "undefined"
console.log(typeof null);    // "object" (quirk!)

lessons.title: null vs. undefined
lessons.body: undefined means a variable has been declared but not yet assigned a value. null is a deliberate assignment that represents 'no value.' Think of undefined as 'nothing here yet' and null as 'intentionally empty.' Both are falsy, but they are not the same type.

lessons.title: Objects — the non-primitive
lessons.body: Everything that is not a primitive is an object. Arrays, functions, dates, and regular objects are all objects under the hood. Unlike primitives, objects are mutable and are stored by reference, not by value. To explore data types in depth, see MDN's JavaScript data types and data structures page, and visit the recommended books section for thorough beginner references.
lessons.code:
const colors = ["red", "green"];  // array (object)
const person = { name: "Eve" };   // object

console.log(typeof colors);  // "object"
console.log(typeof person);  // "object"
console.log(Array.isArray(colors)); // true

topics.title: Strings & Template Literals
topics.description: Master creating, combining, and manipulating text in JavaScript with strings and modern template literals.

lessons.title: Creating strings
lessons.body: A string is a sequence of characters wrapped in quotes. You can use single quotes, double quotes, or backticks. Single and double quotes behave identically. Backticks unlock template literals, which we'll cover shortly. Pick one style and be consistent.

lessons.title: Useful string properties and methods
lessons.body: Strings come with built-in properties and methods. The .length property tells you how many characters a string has. Methods like .toUpperCase(), .toLowerCase(), .trim(), .includes(), and .slice() let you inspect and transform strings without changing the original — strings are immutable.
lessons.code:
const msg = "  Hello, JavaScript!  ";

console.log(msg.length);          // 22
console.log(msg.trim());          // "Hello, JavaScript!"
console.log(msg.includes("Java")); // true
console.log(msg.slice(2, 7));     // "Hello"
console.log(msg.toUpperCase());   // "  HELLO, JAVASCRIPT!  "

lessons.title: Template literals & interpolation
lessons.body: Template literals use backticks (`) and allow you to embed expressions inside ${...}. This is called string interpolation. It is far more readable than concatenation with the + operator, especially when you have multiple variables or calculations.

lessons.title: Multi-line strings
lessons.body: Template literals also support multi-line strings naturally — just press Enter inside the backticks. With regular quotes you would need escape characters like backslash-n. This makes template literals ideal for building HTML snippets or formatted output. For a comprehensive look at string handling, read MDN's Text formatting guide and visit the recommended books section for additional learning paths.

topics.title: Numbers & Basic Math
topics.description: Work with JavaScript's number type, arithmetic operators, and the built-in Math object.

lessons.title: One number type to rule them all
lessons.body: Unlike many languages, JavaScript has a single number type that handles both integers and decimals (floating-point). Behind the scenes it uses 64-bit floating point (IEEE 754). You can write integers, decimals, and even scientific notation.

lessons.title: Arithmetic operators
lessons.body: JavaScript supports the standard arithmetic operators: addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), division (/), remainder (%), and exponentiation (**). The remainder operator is sometimes called modulo and is very useful for checking if a number is even or odd.
lessons.code:
console.log(10 + 3);   // 13
console.log(10 - 3);   // 7
console.log(10 * 3);   // 30
console.log(10 / 3);   // 3.3333...
console.log(10 % 3);   // 1 (remainder)
console.log(2 ** 10);  // 1024 (exponentiation)

lessons.title: The Math object
lessons.body: The built-in Math object provides useful constants and functions for more advanced calculations. You don't need to import anything — Math is always available. Common helpers include Math.round(), Math.floor(), Math.ceil(), Math.max(), Math.min(), Math.abs(), and Math.random().
lessons.code:
console.log(Math.round(4.6));    // 5
console.log(Math.floor(4.9));    // 4
console.log(Math.ceil(4.1));     // 5
console.log(Math.max(1, 5, 3));  // 5
console.log(Math.min(1, 5, 3));  // 1
console.log(Math.abs(-7));       // 7
console.log(Math.random());      // 0 to 0.999...

lessons.title: Watch out for floating-point quirks
lessons.body: Because JavaScript uses floating-point math, you will occasionally see tiny rounding errors. The classic example is 0.1 + 0.2, which does not equal exactly 0.3. When precision matters, multiply to integers first, calculate, then divide back. For the full story on numbers, consult MDN's Numbers and dates guide and visit the recommended books section for beginner-friendly deep dives.
lessons.code:
console.log(0.1 + 0.2);          // 0.30000000000000004
console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3);  // false

// Workaround: use toFixed or integer math
console.log((0.1 + 0.2).toFixed(1)); // "0.3"
console.log((1 + 2) / 10);           // 0.3

topics.title: Booleans & Truthy/Falsy
topics.description: Understand true and false values in JavaScript, including the surprising truthy and falsy rules.

lessons.title: Meet the booleans
lessons.body: A boolean is the simplest data type — it can only be true or false. Booleans are the backbone of decision-making in code. Every if statement, every loop condition, and every comparison ultimately produces a boolean.

lessons.title: Comparison operators produce booleans
lessons.body: Comparisons like ===, !==, <, >, <=, and >= all evaluate to true or false. Always prefer strict equality (===) over loose equality (==). Strict equality checks both value and type, while loose equality can perform surprising type conversions.
lessons.code:
console.log(5 > 3);       // true
console.log(5 === "5");   // false (strict — different types)
console.log(5 == "5");    // true  (loose — coerces type)
console.log(10 !== 10);   // false
console.log(1 < 2);       // true

lessons.title: Truthy and falsy values
lessons.body: In JavaScript, every value has a boolean identity. When used in a boolean context (like an if), values are treated as either truthy or falsy. There are exactly six falsy values: false, 0, '' (empty string), null, undefined, and NaN. Everything else is truthy — including empty arrays and empty objects.

lessons.title: Converting to boolean explicitly
lessons.body: You can convert any value to its boolean equivalent using Boolean() or the double-NOT trick (!!). This is useful when you need a clean true/false rather than a truthy/falsy value. Understanding these conversions will save you from subtle bugs. For a thorough overview, read MDN's Truthy and Falsy reference and visit the recommended books section for foundational JavaScript resources.
lessons.code:
console.log(Boolean(1));        // true
console.log(Boolean(0));        // false
console.log(Boolean("hello"));  // true
console.log(Boolean(""));       // false

// Double-NOT shorthand
console.log(!!"text");  // true
console.log(!!0);       // false

topics.title: Operators & Expressions
topics.description: Learn the essential JavaScript operators — assignment, comparison, logical, and the handy ternary operator.

lessons.title: Assignment operators
lessons.body: The basic assignment operator is =. JavaScript also has compound assignment operators that combine an operation with assignment. These are shorthand — they perform the operation and assign the result in one step. They work with arithmetic and even string concatenation.

lessons.title: Equality: == vs. ===
lessons.body: JavaScript has two equality operators. Loose equality (==) converts types before comparing, which leads to surprising results. Strict equality (===) checks both value and type with no conversion. Always use === and !== to avoid unexpected type coercion bugs.
lessons.code:
console.log(1 == "1");    // true  (loose: string → number)
console.log(1 === "1");   // false (strict: different types)
console.log(null == undefined);  // true  (loose: special rule)
console.log(null === undefined); // false (strict: different types)
console.log(0 == false);  // true  (loose)
console.log(0 === false); // false (strict)

lessons.title: Logical operators
lessons.body: The three logical operators are AND (&&), OR (||), and NOT (!). AND returns the first falsy value or the last value if all are truthy. OR returns the first truthy value or the last value if all are falsy. NOT flips a value's truthiness. These operators short-circuit, meaning they stop evaluating as soon as the result is determined.
lessons.code:
console.log(true && "hello");  // "hello" (both truthy → last value)
console.log(false && "hello"); // false   (short-circuits)
console.log("" || "default");  // "default" (first truthy)
console.log("hi" || "default"); // "hi"

console.log(!true);   // false
console.log(!0);      // true

lessons.title: The ternary operator
lessons.body: The ternary operator is a compact if/else on a single line. The syntax is: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse. It is an expression, so it can be used inside assignments, template literals, or function arguments. Keep ternaries simple — if the logic is complex, use a regular if/else instead. For a complete operator reference, see MDN's Expressions and Operators page and visit the recommended books section for structured learning materials.

topics.title: Conditionals: if, else, switch
topics.description: Control the flow of your programs by making decisions with if statements and switch blocks.

lessons.title: The if statement
lessons.body: The if statement runs a block of code only when a condition is true. The condition goes inside parentheses and the code to execute goes inside curly braces. If the condition is falsy, the block is skipped entirely.

lessons.title: if...else and else if
lessons.body: Add an else block to handle the case when the condition is false. Use else if to test additional conditions in sequence. JavaScript evaluates each condition from top to bottom and runs only the first matching block.

lessons.title: The switch statement
lessons.body: switch is useful when you are comparing a single value against many possible matches. Each case is checked with strict equality (===). Always include break to prevent fall-through, and add a default case to handle unexpected values.

lessons.title: Choosing between if and switch
lessons.body: Use if/else when you need to evaluate different expressions or ranges (like score >= 90). Use switch when you are matching one variable against a set of exact values. Both achieve the same goal — pick the one that makes your code clearer. For more patterns and edge cases, see MDN's control flow documentation and visit the recommended books section for practical examples and exercises.

topics.title: For Loops
topics.description: Repeat actions efficiently using JavaScript's for loop and the modern for...of loop.

lessons.title: The classic for loop
lessons.body: The for loop has three parts separated by semicolons: initialization, condition, and update. The initialization runs once before the loop starts. The condition is checked before each iteration — if false, the loop stops. The update runs after each iteration.

lessons.title: Looping through arrays by index
lessons.body: A common use of the for loop is iterating over an array using its index. The loop counter starts at 0 (arrays are zero-indexed) and runs while it is less than the array's length. This gives you access to both the index and the element.

lessons.title: for...of — the modern way
lessons.body: The for...of loop iterates directly over the values of an iterable (arrays, strings, etc.). You don't need to manage an index — it simply gives you each element one at a time. Use for...of when you don't need the index, and the classic for loop when you do.

lessons.title: Nested for loops
lessons.body: You can place one loop inside another to work with multi-dimensional data or generate combinations. The inner loop completes all its iterations for each single iteration of the outer loop. Be mindful of performance — nested loops multiply the number of iterations. For advanced looping patterns and performance tips, explore MDN's Loops and iteration guide and visit the recommended books section for hands-on practice problems.

topics.title: While & Do-While Loops
topics.description: Learn when to use while and do-while loops, and how to control loop execution with break and continue.

lessons.title: The while loop
lessons.body: A while loop repeats a block of code as long as its condition remains true. The condition is checked before each iteration, so if it starts as false the body never runs. Make sure something inside the loop eventually makes the condition false, or you'll get an infinite loop.

lessons.title: The do...while loop
lessons.body: A do...while loop is like while, but the condition is checked after the body runs. This guarantees the body executes at least once. It is useful when you need to perform an action before deciding whether to repeat it.

lessons.title: break and continue
lessons.body: The break statement exits a loop immediately, no matter what the condition says. The continue statement skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next one. Both work in for, while, and do...while loops.

lessons.title: Avoiding infinite loops
lessons.body: An infinite loop happens when the condition never becomes false. Common causes include forgetting to update the loop variable, or accidentally writing a condition that is always true. If your program freezes, an infinite loop is likely the culprit — in the browser console you can press Ctrl+C or close the tab to stop it. For more loop patterns and best practices, review MDN's Loops and iteration reference and visit the recommended books section for guided exercises.

topics.title: Writing Your First Functions
topics.description: Learn how to create reusable blocks of code with function declarations, expressions, and arrow functions.

lessons.title: Function declarations
lessons.body: A function declaration defines a named, reusable block of code. You declare it with the function keyword, a name, parentheses for parameters, and curly braces for the body. Call the function by its name followed by parentheses. Declared functions are hoisted, meaning you can call them before they appear in your code.

lessons.title: Parameters and return values
lessons.body: Parameters are placeholders listed in the function definition. Arguments are the actual values you pass when calling. The return statement sends a value back to the caller and stops the function. If there is no return, the function returns undefined by default.

lessons.title: Arrow functions
lessons.body: Arrow functions are a shorter syntax introduced in ES6. They use => instead of the function keyword. If the body is a single expression, you can omit the curly braces and the return is implicit. Arrow functions are especially popular for short callbacks and inline logic.

lessons.title: Default parameters
lessons.body: You can give parameters default values that kick in when no argument is passed. This prevents undefined from sneaking into your logic. Defaults are defined with = in the parameter list. For a complete tour of functions, including rest parameters and closures, read MDN's Functions guide and visit the recommended books section for structured practice.

topics.title: Scope & Block Scoping
topics.description: Understand where variables live and why scope matters for writing predictable JavaScript code.

lessons.title: What is scope?
lessons.body: Scope determines where a variable is accessible in your code. Think of scope as a boundary — a variable declared inside a boundary is invisible outside it. JavaScript has three main scope levels: global, function, and block. Understanding scope helps you avoid naming conflicts and unexpected bugs.

lessons.title: Global scope
lessons.body: A variable declared outside any function or block lives in the global scope. It can be accessed from anywhere in your code. While convenient, too many global variables make code hard to maintain and can cause naming collisions. Keep globals to a minimum.

lessons.title: Function scope vs. block scope
lessons.body: Variables declared with var are function-scoped — they are visible throughout the entire function, even before their declaration (due to hoisting). Variables declared with let and const are block-scoped — they exist only within the nearest set of curly braces. Block scoping is more predictable and is one of the main reasons to prefer let and const.

lessons.title: The scope chain
lessons.body: When JavaScript looks up a variable, it starts in the current scope and moves outward through each enclosing scope until it finds the variable or reaches the global scope. This lookup path is called the scope chain. Inner functions can access variables from outer functions, but not the other way around. For a deep understanding of scope, closures, and hoisting, explore MDN's guide on Closures and visit the recommended books section for thorough explanations with visual examples.

topics.title: Introduction to Arrays
topics.description: Store and work with ordered collections of data using JavaScript arrays.

lessons.title: Creating arrays
lessons.body: An array is an ordered list of values. You create one with square brackets and separate items with commas. Arrays can hold any mix of types — strings, numbers, booleans, even other arrays. The .length property tells you how many items the array contains.

lessons.title: Accessing and modifying elements
lessons.body: Array elements are accessed by their index, starting at 0. Use bracket notation to read or update a value at a specific position. Accessing an index that doesn't exist returns undefined rather than an error.

lessons.title: Common array methods
lessons.body: Arrays come with powerful built-in methods. Use push() to add to the end, pop() to remove from the end, unshift() to add to the front, and shift() to remove from the front. The includes() method checks if a value exists, and indexOf() returns its position (or -1 if not found).
lessons.code:
const nums = [1, 2, 3];

nums.push(4);       // [1, 2, 3, 4]
nums.unshift(0);    // [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]
nums.pop();         // [0, 1, 2, 3]
nums.shift();       // [1, 2, 3]

console.log(nums.includes(2));  // true
console.log(nums.indexOf(3));   // 2
console.log(nums.indexOf(99));  // -1

lessons.title: Iterating over arrays
lessons.body: You can loop over an array with for, for...of, or the forEach method. forEach calls a function once for each element and is a clean, readable option for simple iterations. As your skills grow, you'll also learn map, filter, and reduce. For a full reference of array methods, consult MDN's Array documentation and visit the recommended books section for exercises that build real muscle memory.

topics.title: Introduction to Objects
topics.description: Learn how to group related data into key-value pairs with JavaScript objects.

lessons.title: Creating objects
lessons.body: An object is a collection of key-value pairs wrapped in curly braces. Keys (also called properties) are strings (or Symbols), and values can be anything — strings, numbers, arrays, even other objects. Objects let you group related data under one name.

lessons.title: Accessing properties
lessons.body: There are two ways to read a property: dot notation and bracket notation. Dot notation is cleaner for known, fixed property names. Bracket notation is required when the key is stored in a variable or contains special characters like spaces.

lessons.title: Adding, updating, and deleting properties
lessons.body: Objects are mutable — you can add new properties, change existing ones, or remove them entirely. Use assignment to add or update. Use the delete operator to remove a property. You can also check whether a property exists using the in operator.

lessons.title: Looping through an object
lessons.body: Use a for...in loop to iterate over an object's keys, or use Object.keys(), Object.values(), and Object.entries() for more control. Object.entries() is especially handy because it gives you both the key and value in each iteration. For more on objects, prototypes, and methods, dive into MDN's Working with Objects guide and visit the recommended books section for project-based learning resources.

topics.title: Debugging with console
topics.description: Go beyond console.log — learn the full suite of console methods to debug your JavaScript faster.

lessons.title: console.log — your best friend
lessons.body: console.log() prints values to the console and is the most common debugging tool. You can pass multiple arguments separated by commas, and they will be printed on the same line. Use it liberally while developing, then remove or reduce logs before shipping code.

lessons.title: console.warn and console.error
lessons.body: console.warn() displays a yellow warning message, and console.error() displays a red error message. They work just like console.log but visually stand out in the browser's DevTools. Use warn for things that are not ideal but not broken, and error for actual failures.

lessons.title: console.table and console.dir
lessons.body: console.table() prints arrays or objects as a formatted table — it makes structured data much easier to scan. console.dir() shows an interactive, expandable tree view of an object's properties. Both are incredibly useful when inspecting complex data during development.

lessons.title: Timing and grouping
lessons.body: console.time() and console.timeEnd() measure how long a block of code takes to run — pass the same label to both. console.group() and console.groupEnd() let you visually nest related logs for cleaner output. Mastering these tools will make your debugging sessions far more productive. For the full list of console methods and advanced debugging techniques, check out MDN's Console API reference and visit the recommended books section for debugging-focused learning resources.

topics.title: Arrays & Array Methods
topics.description: Work with ordered collections using JavaScript arrays and their powerful built-in methods.

lessons.title: Creating arrays
lessons.body: Arrays are ordered lists that can hold any type. They're created with square brackets.
lessons.code:
const nums = [1, 2, 3];
const mixed = [1, "two", true];

lessons.title: map and filter
lessons.body: map transforms every element and returns a new array. filter returns only the elements that pass the test.
lessons.code:
const doubled = [1, 2, 3].map(n => n * 2);   // [2, 4, 6]
const evens   = [1, 2, 3].filter(n => n % 2 === 0); // [2]

lessons.title: reduce
lessons.body: reduce collapses an array to a single value by applying an accumulator function.
lessons.code: const sum = [1, 2, 3].reduce((acc, n) => acc + n, 0); // 6

lessons.title: Spread and destructuring
lessons.body: Spread (...) copies or merges arrays. Destructuring pulls values into named variables. For a deeper dive into array methods, explore MDN's Array reference and visit the recommended books for more practice.

topics.title: Objects In Depth
topics.description: Master JavaScript objects — the building blocks for organizing and structuring your data.

lessons.title: Creating objects
lessons.body: Objects store data as key-value pairs. Keys are strings (or Symbols), and values can be any type. You can create them with object literals.

lessons.title: Accessing and modifying properties
lessons.body: Use dot notation for known keys and bracket notation for dynamic keys. You can add, change, or delete properties at any time.
lessons.code:
console.log(user.name);        // "Alice"
console.log(user["age"]);      // 28
user.email = "alice@test.com"; // add
delete user.isAdmin;           // remove

lessons.title: Computed properties and shorthand
lessons.body: ES6 shorthand lets you skip repeating variable names. Computed property names use an expression inside brackets as the key.

lessons.title: Object static methods
lessons.body: Object.keys, Object.values, and Object.entries let you iterate over an object's contents easily. Object.assign and the spread operator help you merge objects. Check out MDN's Object reference for the full list of static methods, and the recommended books offer great exercises to solidify these concepts.
lessons.code:
const cat = { name: "Milo", age: 3 };
Object.keys(cat);    // ["name", "age"]
Object.values(cat);  // ["Milo", 3]
Object.entries(cat); // [["name","Milo"],["age",3]]

topics.title: Destructuring & Spread
topics.description: Learn how to unpack values from arrays and objects into distinct variables with elegant ES6 syntax.

lessons.title: Object destructuring
lessons.body: Destructuring extracts properties from an object into standalone variables. You can also rename them and set default values.

lessons.title: Array destructuring
lessons.body: Array destructuring assigns elements by position. You can skip elements with empty commas and use rest syntax to collect the remainder.

lessons.title: Spread operator
lessons.body: The spread operator (...) expands an iterable into individual elements. It's perfect for cloning arrays, merging objects, and passing arguments.

lessons.title: Rest parameters in functions
lessons.body: Rest parameters gather remaining arguments into a real array, replacing the old arguments object. This pairs beautifully with destructuring in function signatures. For more patterns and edge cases, MDN's destructuring assignment guide is an excellent deep dive, and the recommended books walk through dozens of real-world examples.

topics.title: String Methods Toolbox
topics.description: Explore the most useful string methods for searching, extracting, and transforming text in JavaScript.

lessons.title: Searching strings
lessons.body: includes checks for a substring and returns a boolean. indexOf returns the position or -1 if not found. startsWith and endsWith check boundaries.
lessons.code:
const msg = "Hello, world!";
msg.includes("world");    // true
msg.indexOf("world");     // 7
msg.startsWith("Hello");  // true
msg.endsWith("!");        // true

lessons.title: Extracting substrings
lessons.body: slice extracts part of a string by start and end index. It supports negative indices to count from the end. split breaks a string into an array using a separator.
lessons.code:
const str = "JavaScript";
str.slice(0, 4);  // "Java"
str.slice(-6);    // "Script"
"a-b-c".split("-"); // ["a", "b", "c"]

lessons.title: Transforming strings
lessons.body: toUpperCase and toLowerCase change casing. trim removes whitespace from both ends. replace swaps the first match; replaceAll swaps every match.
lessons.code:
"  hello  ".trim();              // "hello"
"hello".toUpperCase();           // "HELLO"
"aabbcc".replace("b", "X");      // "aaXbcc"
"aabbcc".replaceAll("b", "X");   // "aaXXcc"

lessons.title: Template literals revisited
lessons.body: Template literals use backticks and ${} for interpolation. They support multi-line strings and expression evaluation inside the braces. For the complete list of string methods, MDN's String reference is your go-to resource, and the recommended books provide practice challenges to master text manipulation.

topics.title: Arrow Functions
topics.description: Understand arrow function syntax, implicit returns, and how they differ from traditional functions.

lessons.title: Arrow syntax basics
lessons.body: Arrow functions provide a shorter syntax for writing functions. They use => instead of the function keyword.

lessons.title: Implicit return
lessons.body: When the function body is a single expression, you can omit the braces and the return keyword. The expression's value is returned automatically.

lessons.title: Arrow functions and this
lessons.body: Arrow functions don't have their own this — they inherit it from the surrounding scope. This makes them ideal for callbacks but unsuitable as object methods that need this to point to the object.

lessons.title: When to use arrows
lessons.body: Use arrow functions for short callbacks, array methods, and anywhere you want lexical this. Stick with regular functions for object methods and constructors. For a thorough comparison between function types, MDN's arrow function expressions page covers all the nuances, and the recommended books include side-by-side examples that make the differences crystal clear.

topics.title: Callbacks & Higher-Order Functions
topics.description: Learn how functions can be passed as arguments and returned from other functions to create powerful abstractions.

lessons.title: Functions as values
lessons.body: In JavaScript, functions are first-class values. You can assign them to variables, store them in arrays, and pass them to other functions just like any other data.

lessons.title: What is a callback
lessons.body: A callback is a function you pass into another function to be called later. The receiving function decides when and how to invoke it.

lessons.title: Writing higher-order functions
lessons.body: A higher-order function either takes a function as an argument, returns a function, or both. You've already used some — map, filter, and reduce are all higher-order functions.

lessons.title: Returning functions
lessons.body: Functions that return other functions enable patterns like closures and factory functions. The returned function remembers variables from its parent scope. To explore advanced patterns like currying and composition, MDN's guide on closures is invaluable, and the recommended books dedicate entire chapters to functional programming techniques.

topics.title: DOM Basics
topics.description: Discover how to select, read, and modify HTML elements using the Document Object Model.

lessons.title: Selecting elements
lessons.body: querySelector returns the first matching element; querySelectorAll returns a NodeList of all matches. Both accept CSS selectors.

lessons.title: Reading and changing content
lessons.body: textContent gets or sets the text inside an element. innerHTML gets or sets the HTML markup. Use textContent when you don't need to parse HTML — it's safer and faster.

lessons.title: Changing styles and classes
lessons.body: classList provides add, remove, toggle, and contains methods for managing CSS classes. The style property lets you set inline styles directly.

lessons.title: Creating and removing elements
lessons.body: createElement makes a new element, appendChild adds it to the DOM, and remove detaches it. You can build entire UIs programmatically this way. For the complete DOM API, MDN's DOM documentation is the definitive reference, and the recommended books provide hands-on projects that bring these methods to life.

topics.title: Handling Events
topics.description: Respond to user interactions like clicks, key presses, and form submissions with JavaScript event handling.

lessons.title: addEventListener
lessons.body: addEventListener attaches a handler function that runs when the specified event fires. Always prefer it over inline on-attributes for cleaner separation of HTML and JS.

lessons.title: The event object
lessons.body: Every handler receives an event object with details about what happened. It includes properties like target (the element that fired the event), type, and methods like preventDefault.

lessons.title: Event delegation
lessons.body: Instead of adding a listener to every child, add one to a parent and use event.target to determine which child was clicked. This is more efficient, especially with dynamic lists.

lessons.title: Removing listeners and common events
lessons.body: To remove a listener, pass the same function reference to removeEventListener. Common events include click, input, keydown, mouseover, and scroll. MDN's event reference catalogs every event type with browser support tables, and the recommended books feature interactive projects that give you plenty of hands-on practice.

topics.title: Error Handling with try/catch
topics.description: Write resilient code by catching and managing errors gracefully with try, catch, and finally.

lessons.title: try/catch basics
lessons.body: Wrap risky code in a try block. If an error is thrown, execution jumps to catch where you can handle it without crashing your program.

lessons.title: The Error object
lessons.body: Errors have a message property describing what went wrong and a name property like TypeError or RangeError. The stack property shows where the error originated.

lessons.title: Throwing custom errors
lessons.body: Use throw to signal an error when a condition isn't met. You can throw any value, but Error objects are preferred because they include a stack trace.

lessons.title: finally and best practices
lessons.body: The finally block runs whether or not an error occurred — perfect for cleanup like closing connections. Keep try blocks small and only catch errors you can meaningfully handle. MDN's error handling guide explains every built-in error type in detail, and the recommended books offer patterns for building robust error-handling strategies in real applications.

topics.title: Working with JSON
topics.description: Parse, create, and manipulate JSON — the universal data format for web APIs and configuration files.

lessons.title: What is JSON
lessons.body: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is a lightweight text format for structuring data. It looks like JavaScript objects but only allows strings as keys and supports strings, numbers, booleans, null, arrays, and objects as values.
lessons.code:
// Valid JSON (note the double-quoted keys)
// { "name": "Ada", "age": 30, "languages": ["JS", "Python"] }

lessons.title: JSON.parse
lessons.body: JSON.parse converts a JSON string into a JavaScript value. It throws a SyntaxError if the input is not valid JSON, so always wrap it in try/catch when parsing untrusted data.

lessons.title: JSON.stringify
lessons.body: JSON.stringify converts a JavaScript value to a JSON string. Pass a number as the third argument to pretty-print with indentation. Functions and undefined values are silently omitted.

lessons.title: Deep cloning and practical tips
lessons.body: A quick way to deep-clone simple objects is structuredClone, though JSON round-tripping also works for plain data. Be aware that JSON cannot represent Dates, Maps, Sets, or functions. For advanced serialization techniques and custom replacers, MDN's JSON documentation covers every option, and the recommended books include real-world API projects that put JSON skills to work.

topics.title: Dates & Time
topics.description: Create, read, and manipulate dates and times using JavaScript's built-in Date object.

lessons.title: Creating dates
lessons.body: The Date constructor creates date objects. With no arguments it gives the current date and time. You can also pass a date string or individual components (year, month index, day).

lessons.title: Reading date components
lessons.body: Use getter methods to extract parts of a date. getFullYear, getMonth (0-based), getDate, getDay (0 = Sunday), getHours, getMinutes, and getSeconds.
lessons.code:
const d = new Date("2024-06-15T10:30:00");
console.log(d.getFullYear()); // 2024
console.log(d.getMonth());    // 5 (June)
console.log(d.getDate());     // 15
console.log(d.getDay());      // 6 (Saturday)

lessons.title: Date arithmetic
lessons.body: Dates are stored as milliseconds since January 1, 1970. You can subtract two dates to get the difference in milliseconds, then convert to days, hours, or minutes.

lessons.title: Formatting dates
lessons.body: toLocaleDateString and toLocaleTimeString format dates according to a locale. Intl.DateTimeFormat gives even more control. Be cautious with date parsing across browsers — always prefer ISO 8601 strings. MDN's Date reference documents every method and locale option, and the recommended books cover libraries like date-fns for more complex date handling.

topics.title: The Math Object
topics.description: Use JavaScript's built-in Math object for rounding, random numbers, and common mathematical operations.

lessons.title: Rounding methods
lessons.body: Math.round rounds to the nearest integer. Math.floor always rounds down, and Math.ceil always rounds up. Math.trunc removes the decimal part entirely.
lessons.code:
Math.round(4.5);  // 5
Math.round(4.4);  // 4
Math.floor(4.9);  // 4
Math.ceil(4.1);   // 5
Math.trunc(-4.7); // -4

lessons.title: Min, max, and random
lessons.body: Math.min and Math.max find the smallest and largest values. Math.random returns a random float between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). Combine them to generate random integers in a range.

lessons.title: Powers, roots, and constants
lessons.body: Math.pow raises a base to an exponent (or use the ** operator). Math.sqrt returns the square root. Math.abs gives the absolute value. Math.PI and Math.E are built-in constants.

lessons.title: Practical examples
lessons.body: Combine Math methods for everyday tasks like clamping values, generating IDs, or computing distances. These patterns come up constantly in UI development and game logic. For a complete list of Math methods including trigonometric functions, MDN's Math reference is comprehensive, and the recommended books include algorithm exercises that put these methods into practice.

topics.title: Sets & Maps
topics.description: Go beyond plain objects and arrays with Set for unique values and Map for flexible key-value storage.

lessons.title: Creating and using Sets
lessons.body: A Set stores unique values of any type. Duplicates are automatically ignored. Use add, has, delete, and size to manage the collection.

lessons.title: Set operations
lessons.body: You can perform union, intersection, and difference operations by combining Sets with spread and filter. These patterns are handy for comparing lists or removing duplicates.

lessons.title: Creating and using Maps
lessons.body: A Map holds key-value pairs where keys can be any type — objects, functions, even NaN. Unlike plain objects, Maps maintain insertion order and have a handy size property.

lessons.title: When to choose Sets and Maps
lessons.body: Use a Set when you need a collection of unique values or want fast lookups. Use a Map when your keys aren't simple strings, or you need guaranteed insertion order. Plain objects are still fine for simple string-keyed data. MDN's Set and Map references detail every method and performance characteristic, and the recommended books contain data-structure challenges that help you choose the right tool for each job.

topics.title: Modules: import & export
topics.description: Organize your code into reusable pieces with JavaScript's native ES module system.

lessons.title: Named exports
lessons.body: Named exports let you share multiple values from a file. Each export is identified by its name and must be imported by the same name (or aliased).

lessons.title: Default exports
lessons.body: A module can have one default export. It's imported without braces and you can name it whatever you like. Default exports are common for classes and main functions.

lessons.title: Importing modules
lessons.body: Use import { name } for named exports and import name for defaults. You can rename imports with as, and import everything with * as namespace.

lessons.title: Organizing code with modules
lessons.body: Group related functions into dedicated files and re-export them through an index file for clean imports. Keep modules focused on a single responsibility. For advanced patterns like dynamic imports and tree shaking, MDN's JavaScript modules guide is a thorough reference, and the recommended books demonstrate module architecture in full-scale projects.

topics.title: Regular Expressions Basics
topics.description: Learn to search, match, and replace text patterns using JavaScript regular expressions.

lessons.title: Creating regex patterns
lessons.body: Regular expressions (regex) define search patterns. In JavaScript, create them with literal slashes or the RegExp constructor. Flags like g (global) and i (case-insensitive) modify behavior.

lessons.title: Testing and matching
lessons.body: test returns true or false for a match. match returns the matched substrings. matchAll (with the g flag) returns an iterator of all matches with capture group details.

lessons.title: Character classes and quantifiers
lessons.body: Character classes match sets of characters: \d (digit), \w (word char), \s (whitespace), . (any char). Quantifiers control repetition: + (one or more), * (zero or more), ? (optional), {n,m} (range).

lessons.title: Practical regex patterns
lessons.body: Use replace with regex for powerful text transformations. Capture groups (parentheses) let you extract or rearrange parts of a match. Even simple patterns can save dozens of lines of string manipulation code. For the full syntax including lookaheads and named groups, MDN's regular expressions guide is the most complete resource, and the recommended books feature regex challenges that build pattern-matching intuition.

topics.title: Promises from Scratch
topics.description: Understand how Promises represent eventual values, chain asynchronous steps, and handle errors gracefully.

lessons.title: Creating a Promise
lessons.body: A Promise wraps an asynchronous operation. You pass a function with resolve and reject callbacks to the Promise constructor. Calling resolve fulfills the promise; calling reject fails it.

lessons.title: Chaining with .then()
lessons.body: Each .then() returns a new Promise, so you can chain transformations. The value returned from one .then() callback becomes the input of the next. This keeps asynchronous code flat instead of nested.
lessons.code:
fetch('https://api.example.com/user/1')
  .then((res) => res.json())
  .then((user) => fetch(`https://api.example.com/posts?userId=${user.id}`))
  .then((res) => res.json())
  .then((posts) => console.log(posts));

lessons.title: Error handling with .catch() and .finally()
lessons.body: A rejected promise skips subsequent .then() handlers and jumps to the nearest .catch(). Use .finally() for cleanup that must run regardless of outcome, such as hiding a loading spinner.
lessons.code:
fetchData()
  .then((data) => process(data))
  .catch((err) => console.error('Failed:', err.message))
  .finally(() => console.log('Done — success or failure'));

lessons.title: Promise combinators
lessons.body: Promise.all waits for every promise to fulfill (or rejects on the first failure). Promise.race resolves or rejects with whichever promise settles first. Promise.allSettled waits for all to settle regardless of outcome, and Promise.any resolves with the first fulfillment. These combinators let you coordinate concurrent work. For a deep dive, read the Promise section on MDN and consult the recommended books for thorough coverage of async patterns.

topics.title: async / await
topics.description: Write asynchronous code that reads like synchronous code using async functions and the await keyword.

lessons.title: The async keyword
lessons.body: Marking a function with async makes it always return a Promise. Any value you return is automatically wrapped in Promise.resolve, and thrown errors become rejections.

lessons.title: await and sequential execution
lessons.body: Inside an async function, await pauses execution until the promise settles, then unwraps its fulfilled value. This lets you write step-by-step logic without .then() chains.

lessons.title: Error handling with try / catch
lessons.body: Wrap awaited calls in try/catch to handle rejections. This mirrors synchronous error handling, making the control flow easier to follow.

lessons.title: Running tasks in parallel
lessons.body: Using await in a loop runs tasks one after another. To run them concurrently, start all promises first and then await them together with Promise.all. This is critical for performance when tasks are independent. For a deep dive, read the async function reference on MDN and visit the recommended books for comprehensive async/await patterns.

topics.title: Closures
topics.description: Learn how closures let inner functions retain access to the outer function's scope.

lessons.title: What is a closure?
lessons.body: A closure is created when a function is defined inside another function and remembers the outer function's variables even after the outer function has returned.

lessons.title: A practical example: counter
lessons.body: The inner function captures the count variable. Each call to the returned function increments the same captured variable.

lessons.title: Closures and loops
lessons.body: A classic gotcha: if you close over a var inside a loop, all closures share the same variable. Use let (block-scoped) to give each iteration its own binding.

lessons.title: When closures shine
lessons.body: Closures power data privacy, factory functions, and partial application. They are one of JavaScript's most powerful patterns. For a deep dive, read the Closures chapter on MDN and visit the recommended books for thorough coverage.

topics.title: Prototypes & the Prototype Chain
topics.description: Discover how JavaScript objects delegate property lookups through the prototype chain.

lessons.title: Every object has a prototype
lessons.body: When you access a property on an object, JavaScript first looks at the object itself. If the property is not found, it follows the internal [[Prototype]] link to the next object in the chain, continuing until it reaches null.

lessons.title: Constructor functions and .prototype
lessons.body: When you call a function with new, the new object's [[Prototype]] is set to the constructor's .prototype property. This is how methods are shared across all instances without duplicating them.

lessons.title: Inspecting the chain
lessons.body: Use Object.getPrototypeOf() to read an object's prototype. Use hasOwnProperty() or Object.hasOwn() to check whether a property lives directly on the object rather than being inherited.
lessons.code:
const obj = { a: 1 };
console.log(Object.getPrototypeOf(obj) === Object.prototype); // true
console.log(obj.hasOwnProperty('a'));       // true
console.log(obj.hasOwnProperty('toString')); // false (inherited)

lessons.title: Prototype pollution and best practices
lessons.body: Modifying Object.prototype affects every object in the runtime—never do it in production code. Prefer Object.create(null) for dictionary-like objects when you need a clean prototype. Understanding the chain is essential for debugging and performance. For a deep dive, read the Inheritance and the prototype chain guide on MDN and consult the recommended books for expert-level treatment.

topics.title: Classes & Inheritance
topics.description: Use ES6 class syntax to define constructors, methods, static members, and inheritance hierarchies.

lessons.title: Defining a class
lessons.body: The class keyword is syntactic sugar over prototype-based inheritance. A class body contains a constructor and method definitions. Unlike function declarations, classes are not hoisted.

lessons.title: Inheritance with extends and super
lessons.body: Use extends to create a subclass. The super keyword calls the parent constructor and lets you invoke parent methods from overridden ones.

lessons.title: Static methods and private fields
lessons.body: Static methods belong to the class itself, not instances. Private fields (prefixed with #) are truly private—accessible only inside the class body, enforced by the engine.

lessons.title: Getters, setters, and design tips
lessons.body: Getters and setters let you add validation or computed values behind a property access syntax. Favor composition over deep inheritance trees—two levels is usually enough. Classes are a convenience layer; the prototype system does the real work underneath. For a deep dive, read the Classes reference on MDN and visit the recommended books for in-depth coverage of object-oriented JavaScript.

topics.title: The 'this' Keyword Demystified
topics.description: Master the rules that determine what 'this' refers to in every calling context.

lessons.title: Default and implicit binding
lessons.body: In non-strict mode, a standalone function call sets this to the global object (window in browsers, globalThis in Node). When a function is called as a method of an object, this refers to the object before the dot.

lessons.title: Explicit binding: call, apply, bind
lessons.body: call and apply invoke a function immediately with a specified this value. call takes arguments individually, apply takes an array. bind returns a new function permanently bound to the given this value.

lessons.title: Arrow functions inherit this
lessons.body: Arrow functions do not have their own this. They capture this from the enclosing lexical scope at the time they are defined. This makes them ideal for callbacks where you want to preserve the outer this.

lessons.title: Common pitfalls and the priority order
lessons.body: The binding priority from highest to lowest is: new binding, explicit binding (call/apply/bind), implicit binding (method call), and default binding. Losing the receiver when extracting a method is the most common mistake. Use bind or arrow functions to lock in context. For a deep dive, read the this reference on MDN and visit the recommended books for a rigorous exploration of JavaScript's execution context.

topics.title: Iterators & Generators
topics.description: Build custom iterables with the iteration protocol and produce values lazily with generator functions.

lessons.title: The iteration protocol
lessons.body: An object is iterable if it has a [Symbol.iterator]() method that returns an iterator. An iterator must have a next() method returning { value, done }. for...of, spread, and destructuring all rely on this protocol.

lessons.title: Generator functions
lessons.body: A function* creates a generator that pauses at each yield and resumes when next() is called. Generators are iterators automatically, making custom iteration much simpler.

lessons.title: yield* and composing generators
lessons.body: yield* delegates to another iterable or generator, flattening its values into the outer sequence. This lets you compose complex iteration logic from simple building blocks.

lessons.title: Lazy evaluation and async generators
lessons.body: Generators compute values on demand, making them memory-efficient for large or infinite sequences. Async generators (async function*) combine generators with await, letting you yield values from asynchronous sources like paginated APIs. For a deep dive, read the Iterators and Generators guide on MDN and consult the recommended books for advanced lazy evaluation techniques.

topics.title: Symbols & Well-Known Symbols
topics.description: Explore symbols as unique, collision-free property keys and learn the well-known symbols that customize language behavior.

lessons.title: Creating and using symbols
lessons.body: Symbol() creates a unique, immutable value every time. The optional description string is for debugging only—two symbols with the same description are never equal.

lessons.title: Symbols as property keys
lessons.body: Symbols can be used as object property keys. They do not appear in for...in loops or Object.keys(), providing a form of soft privacy that avoids name collisions with string keys.

lessons.title: Well-known symbols
lessons.body: JavaScript defines several well-known symbols that let you customize built-in behavior. Symbol.iterator makes an object iterable. Symbol.toPrimitive controls type coercion. Symbol.hasInstance customizes instanceof checks.

lessons.title: The global symbol registry
lessons.body: Symbol.for(key) returns a shared symbol from a global registry, creating it if needed. Symbol.keyFor(sym) retrieves the key. This lets separate modules share the same symbol identity. Use the registry when cross-realm or cross-module symbol sharing is needed; use plain Symbol() for local uniqueness. For a deep dive, read the Symbol reference on MDN and check the recommended books for advanced metaprogramming techniques.

topics.title: Proxy & Reflect
topics.description: Intercept and customize fundamental object operations using Proxy traps and the Reflect API.

lessons.title: Creating a Proxy
lessons.body: A Proxy wraps a target object with a handler that defines traps—functions that intercept operations like property access, assignment, and deletion.

lessons.title: Validation with set and get traps
lessons.body: The set trap runs before a property is assigned, letting you validate or transform values. The get trap can provide default values for missing properties or log access patterns.

lessons.title: The Reflect API
lessons.body: Reflect provides static methods that mirror every Proxy trap. Using Reflect inside a trap preserves correct default behavior and returns a boolean success indicator instead of throwing.

lessons.title: Real-world patterns and revocable proxies
lessons.body: Proxy.revocable() creates a proxy that can be permanently disabled, useful for granting temporary access to an object. Proxies power reactive state systems in frameworks like Vue 3 and are used for auto-memoization, API mocking, and access-control layers. For a deep dive, read the Proxy and Reflect references on MDN and explore the recommended books for advanced metaprogramming.

topics.title: WeakMap, WeakSet & WeakRef
topics.description: Use weak references to associate data with objects without preventing garbage collection.

lessons.title: WeakMap basics
lessons.body: A WeakMap holds key-value pairs where keys must be objects (or non-registered symbols). Keys are held weakly, meaning if no other reference to the key exists, the entry can be garbage-collected.

lessons.title: WeakSet for tagging objects
lessons.body: A WeakSet stores a collection of objects with no values. It is ideal for tagging or tracking objects—such as marking them as visited—without creating memory leaks.

lessons.title: WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry
lessons.body: WeakRef creates a weak reference to an object. Call deref() to access it—if the object was collected, deref() returns undefined. FinalizationRegistry lets you register a cleanup callback that fires after an object is garbage-collected.

lessons.title: When to use (and avoid) weak references
lessons.body: WeakMap is perfect for caching metadata about DOM nodes, attaching private data to objects, or memoization caches that should not prevent GC. WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry are advanced tools—avoid relying on them for correctness because garbage collection timing is unpredictable. For a deep dive, read the WeakMap, WeakSet, and WeakRef references on MDN and explore the recommended books for memory management strategies.

topics.title: Custom Errors & Error Subclasses
topics.description: Create meaningful custom error types by extending the built-in Error class for precise error handling.

lessons.title: Built-in error types
lessons.body: JavaScript provides several built-in error constructors: TypeError, RangeError, ReferenceError, SyntaxError, and URIError. Each sets a name and message. Understanding them helps you throw the right type and catch errors selectively.

lessons.title: Creating custom error classes
lessons.body: Extend Error to create your own error types. Set this.name to your class name so stack traces are readable. Custom errors let calling code use instanceof to distinguish different failure modes.

lessons.title: Enriching errors with metadata
lessons.body: Attach a cause via the options bag to chain errors. Add status codes, error codes, or context data as properties so error handlers can make decisions without parsing message strings.

lessons.title: Catching by type and best practices
lessons.body: Use instanceof checks inside catch blocks to handle different error types differently. Group related errors under a common base class for coarse-grained catching. Always re-throw errors you do not know how to handle. For a deep dive, read the Error reference on MDN and consult the recommended books for robust error handling architecture.

topics.title: Fetch API & HTTP Requests
topics.description: Make HTTP requests from the browser with the Fetch API, handle responses, and manage cancellation.

lessons.title: Basic GET requests
lessons.body: fetch() returns a Promise that resolves to a Response object. The promise resolves even on HTTP error statuses (404, 500), so always check response.ok before reading the body.

lessons.title: POST and request options
lessons.body: Pass a second argument to fetch with method, headers, and body. For JSON payloads, set the Content-Type header and stringify the body. Other methods (PUT, PATCH, DELETE) work the same way.

lessons.title: Response types and streaming
lessons.body: A Response has methods for different formats: .json(), .text(), .blob(), .arrayBuffer(), and .formData(). The body is a ReadableStream, so you can process large payloads incrementally using the reader interface.

lessons.title: Cancellation with AbortController
lessons.body: Create an AbortController and pass its signal to fetch. Calling controller.abort() rejects the fetch promise with an AbortError. This is essential for cancelling requests when a component unmounts or a user navigates away. For a deep dive, read the Fetch API and AbortController references on MDN and check the recommended books for production-ready networking patterns.

topics.title: Web Storage: localStorage & sessionStorage
topics.description: Persist key-value data in the browser using the Web Storage API and understand its trade-offs.

lessons.title: localStorage basics
lessons.body: localStorage stores string key-value pairs that persist until explicitly deleted. Data survives page reloads and browser restarts. Use setItem, getItem, and removeItem to manage entries.

lessons.title: sessionStorage and its differences
lessons.body: sessionStorage has the same API as localStorage but its data is scoped to the browser tab. It is cleared when the tab is closed. Use sessionStorage for temporary state like form drafts or wizard progress.

lessons.title: Storing complex data with JSON
lessons.body: Web Storage only stores strings. Wrap objects and arrays with JSON.stringify on write and JSON.parse on read. Always handle potential parse errors for corrupted data.

lessons.title: Limits, security, and the storage event
lessons.body: Most browsers cap Web Storage at about 5–10 MB per origin. Data is stored unencrypted, so never store tokens or secrets. The storage event fires on other tabs when data changes, enabling simple cross-tab communication. For larger or structured data, consider IndexedDB. For a deep dive, read the Web Storage API reference on MDN and explore the recommended books for client-side data persistence strategies.

topics.title: Timers, Intervals & requestAnimationFrame
topics.description: Schedule code execution with setTimeout, setInterval, and requestAnimationFrame, and learn debounce and throttle patterns.

lessons.title: setTimeout and clearTimeout
lessons.body: setTimeout schedules a callback to run after at least the specified delay in milliseconds. It returns a timer ID you can pass to clearTimeout to cancel the pending execution. The actual delay may be longer due to the event loop.

lessons.title: setInterval and clearInterval
lessons.body: setInterval repeatedly calls a callback at the given interval. Always store the ID and call clearInterval when done to prevent leaks—especially in single-page apps where components mount and unmount.

lessons.title: requestAnimationFrame
lessons.body: requestAnimationFrame schedules a callback before the browser's next repaint, typically at 60 fps. It is the correct way to drive visual animations because the browser can optimize it—pausing when the tab is hidden.

lessons.title: Debounce and throttle patterns
lessons.body: Debounce delays execution until a pause in events (e.g., typing stops). Throttle ensures a function runs at most once per interval (e.g., scroll handling). Both use setTimeout internally and are essential for performance. For a deep dive, read the setTimeout and requestAnimationFrame references on MDN and consult the recommended books for advanced scheduling and animation techniques.

topics.title: Functional Patterns: Curry, Compose, Pipe
topics.description: Apply functional programming techniques—currying, composition, and piping—to write declarative, reusable JavaScript.

lessons.title: Currying
lessons.body: Currying transforms a function that takes multiple arguments into a chain of functions each taking one argument. This lets you fix some arguments up front and reuse the partially applied function with different remaining arguments.

lessons.title: Partial application
lessons.body: Partial application fixes one or more arguments of a function, returning a new function that accepts the rest. Unlike currying, partial application does not require one-argument-at-a-time calling.

lessons.title: Compose and pipe
lessons.body: Composition combines functions so the output of one becomes the input of the next. compose applies functions right-to-left; pipe applies them left-to-right. Pipe usually reads more naturally.

lessons.title: Building pipelines in practice
lessons.body: Functional pipelines shine when processing data through a series of transformations. Each step is a small, testable, reusable function. Combine pipe with curried helpers to build expressive data flows without intermediate variables. For a deep dive, read the Array methods and functional programming sections on MDN and explore the recommended books for comprehensive functional JavaScript patterns.

topics.title: Event Loop & Microtask Queue
topics.description: Understand how JavaScript's single-threaded event loop, call stack, and task queues work together.

lessons.title: Call stack
lessons.body: JavaScript executes synchronous code on a single call stack — one thing at a time. A function call pushes a frame; returning pops it.

lessons.title: Task queue (macrotasks)
lessons.body: setTimeout and setInterval schedule callbacks in the macrotask queue. The event loop picks one macrotask per loop iteration after the call stack is empty.

lessons.title: Microtask queue
lessons.body: Promise callbacks (.then, .catch, await) go into the microtask queue. All microtasks are drained before the next macrotask runs.

lessons.title: Practical implications
lessons.body: Heavy synchronous work blocks the event loop. Break long tasks with setTimeout(fn, 0) or requestIdleCallback to keep the UI responsive. For a comprehensive deep dive, read the MDN Event Loop article and visit the recommended books for expert-level coverage.

topics.title: Memory Management & Garbage Collection
topics.description: Learn how JavaScript engines allocate, track, and reclaim memory, and how to avoid leaks in real-world applications.

lessons.title: Heap & stack allocation
lessons.body: Primitives live on the stack; objects, arrays, and closures are heap-allocated. The engine tracks references from stack roots, global scope, and active closures to determine what is reachable.

lessons.title: Mark-and-sweep & generational GC
lessons.body: Modern engines use mark-and-sweep: starting from roots, they mark every reachable object and sweep (free) the rest. V8 splits the heap into a young generation (short-lived objects, Scavenge collector) and an old generation (long-lived, Mark-Compact). Frequent minor GCs handle the young gen cheaply; major GCs compact the old gen less often.

lessons.title: Common memory leaks
lessons.body: Detached DOM nodes, forgotten event listeners, closures capturing large scopes, unbounded caches, and uncleared timers are the most common leak sources. WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry let you hold non-preventing references and run cleanup when objects are collected.

lessons.title: Profiling memory in practice
lessons.body: Use Chrome DevTools' Memory tab to take heap snapshots, record allocation timelines, and compare retained sizes. The 'Detached HTMLElement' filter catches DOM leaks quickly. For the full specification on WeakRef and FinalizationRegistry semantics, consult the TC39 proposal docs and MDN Memory Management guide, and see the recommended books for expert-level coverage of engine internals.

topics.title: Web Workers & Multithreading
topics.description: Offload heavy computation to background threads using Web Workers and SharedArrayBuffer for true parallelism.

lessons.title: Dedicated Workers
lessons.body: A dedicated Worker runs a separate JS file on its own thread with its own global scope (no DOM access). Communication happens via postMessage/onmessage using the structured clone algorithm. Workers are ideal for CPU-bound tasks like image processing or data parsing.

lessons.title: Transferable objects
lessons.body: Structured cloning copies data, which is slow for large buffers. Transferable objects (ArrayBuffer, MessagePort, OffscreenCanvas) move ownership to the worker in O(1) time — the sender loses access immediately.

lessons.title: SharedArrayBuffer & Atomics
lessons.body: SharedArrayBuffer allows multiple threads to read and write the same memory. Atomics provides atomic operations (load, store, add, compareExchange) and wait/notify for lock-free synchronization. Requires cross-origin isolation headers (COOP/COEP).

lessons.title: Worker pools & best practices
lessons.body: Spawning a worker per task is expensive. Maintain a pool of long-lived workers and dispatch tasks via a message queue. navigator.hardwareConcurrency tells you how many logical cores are available. For full details on the Worker API, SharedArrayBuffer security model, and cross-origin isolation, consult the MDN Web Workers guide and the HTML Living Standard, and see the recommended books for production-grade multithreading patterns.

topics.title: Service Workers & Offline Caching
topics.description: Master service worker lifecycles, caching strategies, and background sync to build resilient offline-first web apps.

lessons.title: Lifecycle: install, activate, fetch
lessons.body: A service worker goes through registration → install → activate. During install, precache critical assets. During activate, clean old caches. The fetch event intercepts every network request from controlled pages, letting you serve cached responses or fall back to the network.

lessons.title: Caching strategies
lessons.body: Cache-first serves from cache, falling back to network (fast, but stale). Network-first tries the network, falling back to cache (fresh, but slower offline). Stale-while-revalidate serves cache immediately and updates it in the background — the best balance for most apps.

lessons.title: Background sync & periodic sync
lessons.body: The Background Sync API lets you defer actions (like sending a form) until the user has connectivity. Register a sync tag, then handle the 'sync' event in the service worker. Periodic Background Sync (with permission) lets you refresh content on a schedule even when the page is closed.

lessons.title: Update flow & advanced patterns
lessons.body: When the browser detects a byte-different service worker, it installs the new version but waits for all controlled tabs to close before activating it. Call skipWaiting() to activate immediately and clients.claim() to take control of open tabs. For the complete lifecycle specification, cache storage limits, and push notification integration, see the MDN Service Worker API docs and the Service Worker spec, and see the recommended books for in-depth offline-first architecture patterns.

topics.title: Streams API
topics.description: Process data incrementally with ReadableStream, WritableStream, and TransformStream for memory-efficient I/O.

lessons.title: ReadableStream fundamentals
lessons.body: A ReadableStream represents an asynchronous source of data delivered in chunks. The underlying source pushes chunks via a controller. Consumers read using a reader or pipe the stream. Backpressure is built in — the source is signaled to slow down when the internal queue is full.

lessons.title: WritableStream & piping
lessons.body: A WritableStream consumes chunks. Its underlying sink defines write(), close(), and abort() methods. Use pipeThrough() to route a readable stream through a TransformStream, and pipeTo() to send it to a writable stream. The pipe chain handles backpressure automatically across all stages.

lessons.title: TransformStream
lessons.body: TransformStream sits between a readable and writable side, transforming chunks in flight. This is ideal for on-the-fly decompression, parsing line-delimited JSON, or encoding conversions. TextDecoderStream and CompressionStream are built-in transform streams available in modern browsers.

lessons.title: Real-world streaming patterns
lessons.body: Streams shine for large file uploads (slice into chunks), server-sent event parsing, and streaming JSON from APIs without buffering the full response. Combining ReadableStream.from() with async iterables bridges the gap with for-await-of loops. For the complete Streams specification, backpressure algorithms, and byte streams, consult the WHATWG Streams Standard and MDN Streams API docs, and see the recommended books for advanced data pipeline patterns.

topics.title: Typed Arrays & ArrayBuffer
topics.description: Work with raw binary data efficiently using ArrayBuffer, DataView, and typed array views for graphics, audio, and network protocols.

lessons.title: ArrayBuffer & views
lessons.body: An ArrayBuffer is a fixed-length block of raw bytes. You cannot read or write it directly — you need a view. Typed arrays (Uint8Array, Float32Array, etc.) provide indexed access with a fixed element type. Multiple views can overlay the same buffer to interpret bytes differently.

lessons.title: DataView for mixed formats
lessons.body: DataView lets you read and write arbitrary types at arbitrary byte offsets with explicit endianness control. This is essential for parsing binary protocols, file formats (PNG, WAV), and network packets where fields have mixed sizes and byte orders.

lessons.title: Typed array methods & slicing
lessons.body: Typed arrays share many Array methods (map, filter, slice, sort) but always return typed arrays, not plain arrays. The subarray() method creates a new view over the same buffer (no copy), while slice() copies bytes into a new buffer. Use .buffer, .byteOffset, and .byteLength to navigate the underlying memory.

lessons.title: Performance & interop patterns
lessons.body: Typed arrays are the bridge between JavaScript and low-level APIs: WebGL buffers, Web Audio, WebSocket binary frames, and WebAssembly memory are all backed by ArrayBuffers. Prefer typed arrays over regular arrays when processing large numeric datasets for cache-friendly access and reduced GC pressure. For the full specification of typed arrays, endianness rules, and detached buffer behavior, see the ECMAScript spec and MDN Typed Arrays guide, and see the recommended books for expert binary data techniques.

topics.title: Decorators (Stage 3)
topics.description: Use the TC39 Stage 3 decorator proposal to declaratively modify classes, methods, and accessors with reusable metadata.

lessons.title: What decorators are
lessons.body: A decorator is a function that receives a value (class, method, accessor, or field) and its context, and can replace or augment it. Decorators use the @ syntax above the target. The Stage 3 proposal standardizes a pattern long used via TypeScript experimental decorators and Babel plugins, but with a different, simpler API.

lessons.title: Decorator kinds & context
lessons.body: Decorators can target classes, methods, getters, setters, fields, and auto-accessors. The context object provides kind, name, isStatic, isPrivate, and addInitializer(). The addInitializer hook lets you run setup code when the class or instance is created, which is perfect for registering metadata without modifying the value.

lessons.title: Composing decorators
lessons.body: Multiple decorators apply bottom-up (closest to the target runs first). Decorator factories — functions that return decorators — let you parameterize behavior. Compose small, single-purpose decorators into powerful pipelines: validation, caching, access control, and logging can all stack cleanly.

lessons.title: Auto-accessors & the road ahead
lessons.body: The accessor keyword creates a private backing field with auto-generated get/set, which decorators can intercept for reactive patterns or validation. As decorators advance toward Stage 4, decorator metadata (a companion proposal) will provide a standard way to attach and query metadata. For the latest proposal text, semantics, and migration guides from legacy TypeScript decorators, see the TC39 decorators proposal on GitHub and MDN documentation, and see the recommended books for decorator design patterns in large-scale applications.

topics.title: Metaprogramming with Proxy & Reflect
topics.description: Intercept and customize fundamental object operations using Proxy traps and Reflect methods for powerful abstractions.

lessons.title: Proxy basics & traps
lessons.body: A Proxy wraps a target object with a handler that defines traps — functions that intercept fundamental operations like get, set, has, deleteProperty, and apply. When a trap is not defined, the operation passes through to the target unchanged. There are 13 traps covering all internal object operations.

lessons.title: Reflect API
lessons.body: Reflect provides static methods that mirror every Proxy trap (Reflect.get, Reflect.set, Reflect.has, etc.). Always use Reflect inside traps to forward operations correctly — it handles receiver binding and returns booleans instead of throwing, making trap logic cleaner and less error-prone than direct object operations.

lessons.title: Practical patterns
lessons.body: Proxies enable validation layers, observable objects (react to changes), negative array indexing, auto-vivification (auto-create nested objects on access), and virtual/lazy properties. Revocable proxies (Proxy.revocable) let you cut off access entirely, useful for capability-based security.

lessons.title: Invariants, performance & caveats
lessons.body: Proxy traps must respect invariants enforced by the engine — for example, you cannot report a non-configurable property as non-existent. Proxies add overhead to every trapped operation, so avoid them on hot paths. Use them at API boundaries, not inside tight loops. For the full list of invariants, trap signatures, and Reflect method semantics, see the ECMAScript specification chapters on Proxy and Reflect and the MDN Proxy reference, and see the recommended books for metaprogramming design patterns and performance analysis.

topics.title: Concurrency Patterns
topics.description: Master advanced async coordination with Promise combinators, semaphores, cancellation, and structured concurrency.

lessons.title: Promise combinators
lessons.body: Promise.all rejects on the first failure. Promise.allSettled waits for every outcome. Promise.race resolves or rejects with the first settled promise. Promise.any resolves with the first fulfilled value and only rejects if all fail (AggregateError). Choosing the right combinator is key to correct concurrent logic.

lessons.title: Limiting concurrency
lessons.body: Firing thousands of requests at once can overwhelm servers and exhaust resources. A semaphore or pool pattern limits how many tasks run concurrently. The pattern: maintain a Set of active promises, wait with Promise.race when the set is full, and remove each task as it settles.

lessons.title: Async iterators & generators
