lessons.body: Async generators (async function*) let you yield values over time, consumed with for-await-of. This is perfect for paginated APIs, streaming data, or any sequence produced asynchronously. Combined with concurrency limits, they form powerful data pipelines.

lessons.title: Structured concurrency & error boundaries
lessons.body: Structured concurrency ensures that spawned async tasks do not outlive their parent scope — if one fails, siblings are cancelled and errors propagate predictably. Combine AbortController with Promise combinators to build error boundaries: cancel all in-flight requests when one fails. For the complete async iteration protocol, Promise combinator specifications, and emerging structured concurrency proposals, consult the TC39 proposals, ECMAScript spec, and MDN async guides, and see the recommended books for production-grade concurrency architecture.

topics.title: How JS Engines Optimize Code
topics.description: Peek behind the curtain at parsing, JIT compilation, inline caches, hidden classes, and deoptimization in modern engines.

lessons.title: Parsing & the AST
lessons.body: The engine first parses source into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). V8 uses lazy parsing: it only fully parses functions when they are first called, reducing startup time. Pre-parsing checks for syntax errors and collects scope info. Avoid patterns that force eager parsing (like wrapping IIFEs around large code) to keep startup fast.

lessons.title: Hidden classes & inline caches
lessons.body: V8 assigns a hidden class (called a Map internally) to every object. Objects with the same property layout share a hidden class, enabling fast property access via inline caches (ICs). Adding properties in different orders creates different hidden classes, defeating ICs and slowing access. Always initialize all properties in the constructor in the same order.

lessons.title: JIT tiers & optimization
lessons.body: V8 starts with the Ignition interpreter (fast startup, slow execution), then promotes hot functions to Sparkplug (baseline compiler), then Maglev (mid-tier), and finally TurboFan (optimizing compiler). TurboFan uses type feedback from ICs to speculate — inlining calls, eliminating type checks, and unrolling loops. Speculation failure triggers deoptimization: the engine drops back to interpreted code and re-profiles.

lessons.title: Avoiding deoptimization
lessons.body: Keep functions monomorphic (always pass the same types). Avoid arguments object manipulation, with statements, eval, try-catch around hot code (older engines), and deleting properties. Use TypedArrays for numeric work. Profile with V8's --trace-opt and --trace-deopt flags or Chrome DevTools Performance panel. For deep engine internals, read the V8 blog, the MDN JIT compilation overview, JavaScriptCore architecture docs, and SpiderMonkey documentation, and see the recommended books for comprehensive JIT compiler theory and optimization techniques.

topics.title: WebAssembly Interop
topics.description: Call compiled C/C++/Rust code from JavaScript using WebAssembly, exchange data efficiently, and integrate Wasm modules into web apps.

lessons.title: Loading & instantiating modules
lessons.body: WebAssembly.instantiateStreaming compiles and instantiates a .wasm file in one step, leveraging streaming compilation. The imports object provides functions the Wasm module can call back into JS. The instance's exports object exposes Wasm functions to JS. Always prefer streaming over fetching the entire buffer first.

lessons.title: Memory: the linear model
lessons.body: Wasm and JS share data through WebAssembly.Memory, a resizable ArrayBuffer. JS reads/writes the buffer using typed array views. Strings, arrays, and structs must be manually serialized into linear memory — there is no automatic object marshaling. Memory can grow at runtime with memory.grow(), but the old buffer detaches so you must re-create views.

lessons.title: Tables, globals & multi-value
lessons.body: WebAssembly.Table holds function references, enabling indirect calls and dynamic dispatch (critical for virtual method tables in C++). WebAssembly.Global shares mutable scalar values between JS and Wasm. Multi-value returns let Wasm functions return tuples without memory allocation. These primitives bridge the gap between Wasm's low-level model and JS's dynamic nature.

lessons.title: Wasm-JS integration patterns
lessons.body: For complex data exchange, use a serialization layer or shared typed array conventions. Tools like wasm-bindgen (Rust) and Emscripten (C/C++) generate JS glue code automatically. WASI provides a standard system interface for server-side Wasm. Profile interop boundaries — frequent JS↔Wasm calls can be slower than batching work on one side. For the full WebAssembly JS API, memory model, and upcoming proposals like GC and threads, consult the WebAssembly spec and MDN WebAssembly docs, and see the recommended books for end-to-end Wasm application architecture.

topics.title: Structured Clone & Serialization
topics.description: Deep-copy complex objects with structuredClone, understand the structured clone algorithm, and master serialization boundaries.

lessons.title: structuredClone basics
lessons.body: structuredClone() creates a deep copy of a value using the structured clone algorithm. Unlike JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x)), it handles Date, RegExp, Map, Set, ArrayBuffer, Error, and circular references. It is available globally in browsers, Node.js 17+, and Deno.

lessons.title: Circular references & transferables
lessons.body: The algorithm handles circular references by tracking visited objects during serialization. structuredClone also accepts a transfer list — ArrayBuffers and other transferable objects in the list are moved (zero-copy) to the clone, and the originals are detached.

lessons.title: What cannot be cloned
lessons.body: Functions, DOM nodes, symbols, WeakMap, WeakSet, and objects with prototype chains (class instances lose their prototype) are not cloneable. Property descriptors, getters, setters, and non-enumerable properties are also lost. Understanding these limits prevents subtle bugs when passing objects between workers or caching with structuredClone.

lessons.title: Serialization contexts & alternatives
lessons.body: The structured clone algorithm is used by postMessage, IndexedDB, Cache API, history.pushState, and BroadcastChannel — not just structuredClone(). For cross-origin or network serialization, JSON remains the standard. For richer serialization, consider libraries that handle class revival. For the full algorithm specification, cloneable types list, and transfer semantics, see the HTML Living Standard structured clone section and MDN structuredClone docs, and see the recommended books for serialization design patterns across distributed systems.

topics.title: AbortController & Cancellation
topics.description: Cancel fetch requests, event listeners, and custom async operations cleanly using AbortController and AbortSignal.

lessons.title: AbortController & AbortSignal
lessons.body: AbortController creates an AbortSignal that can be passed to cancellable APIs. Calling controller.abort() sets signal.aborted to true and dispatches an 'abort' event. Fetch, addEventListener, and many newer APIs accept a signal option. Aborting a fetch rejects its promise with an AbortError.

lessons.title: AbortSignal.timeout & AbortSignal.any
lessons.body: AbortSignal.timeout(ms) creates a signal that auto-aborts after a duration — perfect for request timeouts without manual setTimeout. AbortSignal.any([signal1, signal2]) creates a composite signal that aborts when any input signal fires, enabling layered cancellation (user cancel + timeout + page unload).

lessons.title: Cancelling custom async work
lessons.body: Any async function can accept an AbortSignal and check signal.aborted or listen for the abort event. This lets you build cancellable pipelines: polling loops, streaming reads, batch processing, and recursive crawlers can all cooperate with a single controller.

lessons.title: Patterns & integration with event listeners
lessons.body: Pass { signal } to addEventListener to auto-remove listeners when aborted — no need for manual removeEventListener. This is excellent for component lifecycle cleanup. AbortController also integrates with Node.js streams, child_process, and the EventTarget API. For the full AbortController specification, supported APIs, and reason property for abort causes, see the DOM Living Standard and MDN AbortController guide, and see the recommended books for advanced cancellation and resource cleanup patterns.

topics.title: Internationalization with Intl
topics.description: Format numbers, dates, currencies, and lists locale-aware using the built-in Intl API without external libraries.

lessons.title: Intl.NumberFormat
lessons.body: Intl.NumberFormat handles locale-sensitive number formatting: decimals, percentages, currencies, units, and compact notation. It respects locale-specific grouping separators, decimal marks, and currency symbol placement. Create a formatter once and reuse it — construction is expensive, but format() calls are fast.

lessons.title: Intl.DateTimeFormat & Intl.RelativeTimeFormat
lessons.body: Intl.DateTimeFormat formats dates and times with full locale and timezone control. Options let you pick components (weekday, year, month, hour) and styles (long, short, narrow). Intl.RelativeTimeFormat produces human-readable relative strings like '3 days ago' or 'in 2 hours' without manual pluralization logic.

lessons.title: Collator, Segmenter & ListFormat
lessons.body: Intl.Collator provides locale-aware string comparison for sorting — essential for non-ASCII text. Intl.Segmenter splits text into grapheme clusters, words, or sentences correctly (emoji, CJK, and combining characters). Intl.ListFormat joins arrays into grammatically correct lists ('A, B, and C' in English, 'A, B et C' in French).

lessons.title: PluralRules, DisplayNames & best practices
lessons.body: Intl.PluralRules returns the CLDR plural category (zero, one, two, few, many, other) for a number — essential for correct pluralization in languages like Arabic or Polish. Intl.DisplayNames translates language, region, and script codes into human-readable names. Cache formatters, support locale fallback chains, and test with diverse locales. For the complete Intl specification, supported options, and locale negotiation algorithm, see ECMA-402, the Unicode CLDR docs, and MDN Intl reference, and see the recommended books for building fully internationalized applications.

topics.title: Performance Profiling & Optimization
topics.description: Profile, measure, and optimize JavaScript execution using browser APIs, DevTools, and algorithmic best practices.

lessons.title: Performance API & marks
lessons.body: The Performance API provides sub-millisecond timestamps via performance.now() and structured measurement via performance.mark() and performance.measure(). Unlike Date.now(), it is monotonic and not affected by clock adjustments. Use marks to instrument code paths and measures to calculate durations for specific operations.

lessons.title: PerformanceObserver & Web Vitals
lessons.body: PerformanceObserver watches for entries asynchronously — resource loads, long tasks, layout shifts, largest contentful paint, and first input delay. This is the foundation for measuring Core Web Vitals (LCP, FID/INP, CLS) in production. Buffered entries catch events that fired before the observer was registered.

lessons.title: Runtime optimization techniques
lessons.body: Avoid layout thrashing by batching DOM reads and writes. Use requestAnimationFrame for visual updates. Debounce/throttle high-frequency events. Prefer CSS containment and will-change for paint-heavy elements. On the JS side: avoid megamorphic call sites, minimize closures in hot paths, use typed arrays for numeric data, and leverage OffscreenCanvas for heavy rendering.

lessons.title: Profiling workflow & DevTools mastery
lessons.body: Chrome DevTools Performance tab records CPU profiles, flame charts, and rendering timelines. Use 'Bottom-Up' to find the most expensive functions, 'Call Tree' to trace hot call paths, and 'Event Log' for individual events. Enable CPU throttling (4x/6x) to simulate real devices. Lighthouse automates auditing. For the full Performance API specification, observer types, and metric definitions, see the W3C Performance Timeline spec and MDN Performance API docs, and see the recommended books for systematic performance engineering methodology.
